rationale: The cited paper is used in the citing article's core theoretical positioning, not just in a background list. The citing paper specifically attributes to Luffarelli et al. (2019) evidence that logo asymmetry influences brand equity by evoking excitement, and later uses it as prior evidence that logo design can interact with other brand elements. The new paper then develops a parallel logo-design-to-brand-equity framework around descriptiveness, with new mechanism and moderators, indicating a meaningful build-on role.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on the cited study's logo-design and brand-equity line of work by testing a different logo characteristic (descriptiveness) and proposing a different process (processing fluency and authenticity) plus new boundary conditions. It positions its interaction findings as extending prior evidence that logo effects depend on other brand elements.
